nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board, Singapore; U.S. Department of Agriculture
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for this composite dish was found in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values from the input record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-validated against USDA SR Legacy component values for boiled/stir-fried potato (USDA FDC #170026), raw eggplant (USDA FDC #169228), raw long beans/yard-long bean (USDA FDC #169961), vegetable oil, soy sauce, and oyster sauce. The composite per-100g values are consistent with a stir-fried mixed vegetable dish at approximately 40% potato, 30% eggplant, 15% long bean, and 15% sauce/oil/rice contribution. Values retained from input with cross-validation adjustment; confidence remains medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — potato (boiled/stir-fried) and mixed vegetable dish analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate contributors: white rice (GI ~72, University of Sydney GI Database) and boiled/stir-fried potato (GI ~59–65, University of Sydney GI Database). The weighted average of these two high-GI staples, moderated by the fibre and protein contributions of eggplant and long bean, yields an estimated composite GI of approximately 65. Glycemic load per 450 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 69.5) / 100 ≈ 45.2, rounded to 45.4. This is a high GL per serving, consistent with a rice-based meal.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 272.7 mg/100 g from soy sauce and oyster sauce; estimated 1,227 mg sodium per 450 g serving, approaching 50–60% of the 2,000 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and oyster sauce, use low-sodium soy sauce alternatives, and limit portion size to 300 g or less to keep sodium intake below 800 mg per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High estimated GI (~65) and high glycemic load (~45 per 450 g serving) driven by white rice and potato; dietary fibre only 1.45 g/100 g, insufficient to blunt postprandial glucose spike.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute white rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice, reduce potato proportion, increase long bean and eggplant content to raise fibre, and limit serving size to 250–300 g to reduce glycemic load to a manageable level.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 1.09 g/100 g and sodium 272.7 mg/100 g are moderate concerns; trans fat 0.04 g/100 g is low but present; cholesterol 0.91 mg/100 g is negligible.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit portion to 300 g, request cooking with minimal oil, choose low-sodium soy sauce, and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to improve the overall dietary fat and sodium profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Potato, eggplant, and chilli are nightshade vegetables containing solanine, which may aggravate inflammation in RA-sensitive individuals; refined carbohydrate base (white rice) contributes to glycaemic-driven inflammation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: RA patients should monitor individual symptom response to nightshade vegetables, substitute white rice with brown rice, and increase ginger quantity in the dish for its anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Potato, Eggplant and Long Bean (炒土豆茄子豆角) is a Chinese-Singaporean stir-fried vegetable dish typically served as a rice bowl or bento set. The dish combines diced or sliced potato, cubed eggplant (brinjal), and chopped long beans (yard-long beans) stir-fried in vegetable oil with garlic, ginger, soy sauce, oyster sauce, and a light cornstarch slurry to create a glossy, savoury sauce. The potato provides a soft, starchy texture; the eggplant absorbs the sauce and becomes silky and tender; the long beans retain a slight crunch. The flavour profile is umami-forward, mildly salty, and lightly sweet from the oyster sauce. Served over steamed white rice, a standard portion is approximately 450 g. Nutritionally, the dish provides approximately 131 kcal/100 g, with 15.5 g carbohydrate, 6.4 g fat, and 2.7 g protein per 100 g. Sodium is elevated at 272.7 mg/100 g due to soy and oyster sauces. Dietary fibre is modest at 1.45 g/100 g. The dish is vegetarian but not vegan (oyster sauce). Clinically, the combination of white rice and potato creates a high glycemic load per serving (estimated GL ~45 for 450 g), making it a concern for Type 2 Diabetes management. Sodium content is a moderate concern for hypertension. The dish is suitable for general healthy adults in moderate portions but should be modified for metabolic or cardiovascular conditions.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for this composite dish was found in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values were cross-validated against USDA SR Legacy component ingredient data and are consistent with the expected profile of a stir-fried mixed vegetable dish. GI is an analogous estimate based on white rice and potato GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the absence of a direct approved-source match for this specific dish.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific composite stir-fried dish are not available in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. Trans fat is reported as 0.04 g/100 g from the input source (ai_generated, confidence 0.7); this low value is plausible for a dish cooked with vegetable oil and is retained. Cholesterol is 0.91 mg/100 g, consistent with trace amounts from oyster sauce. The dish is classified as vegetarian (no meat or seafood in the primary preparation) but oyster sauce renders it non-vegan; halal status is assumed based on the all-vegetable and plant-oil composition. Two portion sizes were present in the input (450 g and 550 g); 450 g is used as the default serving for GL calculation. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate; a direct GI measurement for this dish has not been published in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of 2024.
